attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 24 weeks gestation with a two-day history of painless vaginal bleeding. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals a gravid uterus consistent with dates and no tenderness. Speculum examination shows a small amount of fresh blood in the vagina and a red, velvety area around the external os of the cervix that bleeds easily when touched.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis in this case is cervical ectropion. This condition is characterized by the presence of endocervical columnar epithelium on the ectocervix, which appears as a red, velvety area around the external os. It is a common finding in pregnancy due to hormonal changes and is often associated with painless vaginal bleeding, especially after intercourse or examination.

Placenta praevia can cause painless antepartum haemorrhage, but it typically presents later in pregnancy and would not be visible on speculum examination. Additionally, the bleeding is usually more significant and not associated with a visible cervical lesion.

Vasa praevia is a rare condition where fetal blood vessels run through the membranes over the internal os. It can cause painless bleeding, but this usually occurs when membranes rupture, leading to fetal blood loss. It would not be visible on speculum examination.

Cervical cancer can present with vaginal bleeding during pregnancy, but it is much less common than cervical ectropion. The appearance of a friable, velvety area is more consistent with ectropion than the typical irregular, ulcerated appearance of cervical cancer.

Placental abruption typically presents with painful vaginal bleeding and uterine tenderness, which are not present in this case. The painless nature of the bleeding and the visible cervical changes make this diagnosis unlikely.

Cervical ectropion is a benign condition that does not typically require treatment during pregnancy. However, it's important to rule out other causes of antepartum haemorrhage. Management usually involves reassurance and avoiding irritation of the cervix. If bleeding persists or becomes heavy, further investigation may be warranted to exclude other pathologies.
learningPoints: Cervical ectropion is a common cause of painless vaginal bleeding during pregnancy.
Cervical ectropion is a benign condition that does not typically require treatment during pregnancy.
Differentiating cervical ectropion from other causes of antepartum haemorrhage is crucial for appropriate management.
references: Aggarwal P, Ben Amor A. Cervical ectropion. In: StatPearls [online]. Updated 2023 May 31. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560709/
Casey PM, Long ME, Marnach ML. Abnormal cervical appearance: what to do, when to worry? Mayo Clin Proc. 2011 Feb;86(2):147-50. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3031439/
